title: Campylobacter qPCR SOP
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"x81d4","text":"To describe the procedures on how to set-up quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) for Campylobacter hipO gene. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":84,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":108,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"xlecn","text":"Scope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bpg68","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e4mhd","text":"In conventional PCR, the amplified DNA product, or amplicon, is detected in an end-point analysis. In real-time PCR, the accumulation of amplification product is measured as the reaction progresses, in real time, with product quantification after each cycle. Real-time detection of PCR products is enabled by the inclusion of a fluorescent reporter molecule in each reaction well that yields increased fluorescence with an increasing amount of product DNA. The fluorescence chemistries employed for this purpose include DNA-binding dyes and fluorescently labeled sequence-specific primers or probes. Specialized thermal cyclers equipped with fluorescence detection modules are used to monitor the fluorescence signal as amplification occurs. The measured fluorescence is proportional to the total amount of amplicon; the change in fluorescence over time is used to calculate the amount of amplicon produced in each cycle. (What is Real-Time PCR (qPCR)? | Bio-Rad)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":923,"length":39}],"data":{}},{"key":"24u4f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c2uee","text":"A probe is a sequence specific oligonucleotide with a reporter dye and a quencher. The chemical reaction behind its mechanism is hydrolysis of a probe (cleavage of the reporter from the quencher), causing the emission of a fluorescent signal. When the probe is intact, the short distance between the reporter and the quencher permits the excitation energy transfer from the reporter to the quencher or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). As a result, the quencher absorbs the light emitted by the reporter. During DNA amplification, the probe binds to the template and Taq DNA Polymerase with its 5’--\u003e3’ exonuclease activity cleaves the probe. Therefore, a light signal is emitted, and qPCR instruments are used to detect the light emitted by the reporter. The light emitted by the reporter corresponds with the amplified DNA. (All about Probe-Based Real-Time qPCR Assays | GoldBio)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":840,"length":53}],"data":{}},{"key":"5afst","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7vrat","text":"This protocol describes the procedures used to quantify Campylobacter in a variety of animal and environmental substrates using qPCR.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6fk14","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5m6sm","text":"Responsibility ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"71acl","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cbqgn","text":"This SOP applies to all staff members and students. These individuals must be knowledgeable about the requirements set forth within this document. The lab manager or designee shall ensure that all staff and students know the proper techniques. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ig2o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"71D74C73EFE24ACAA89FF22D0D62D255","url":"https://www.bio-rad.com/en-in/applications-technologies/what-real-time-pcr-qpcr?ID=LUSO4W8UU"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D6737E706F41455E8677B6584E4CF7D2","url":"https://www.microbiologyresearch.org/content/journal/jmm/10.1099/jmm.0.46909-0"}}}}

steps: If frozen, thaw all reagents and samples and keep on an ice block or ice for stability. If kept in the refrigerator, keep there until ready for use, and keep on an ice block or ice for stability when in use. Standard aliquots (10 8 /5µL– 10 1 /5µL) will be made fresh weekly and kept in the refrigerator to prevent freeze/thaw.
Prepare the qPCR Prep area for setting up a qPCR reaction to eliminate contamination – UV plate, PCR water, and microcentrifuge tubes for 00:15:00 .

Combine the Master Mix components for the number of reactions (allow for 10% extra Master Mix) using the recipe from Table 1 with the correct primer/probe sets ( Table 2 ) for 20 µL reactions. The Gene Expression MasterMix is soapy, so don’t vortex it beforehand. Before adding to the Master Mix - mix the primers and probe by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip. %s %s %s %s
Vortex the completed Master Mix for at least 00:00:20 .
Check the pipette’s volume as you go along, making sure it is exactly at the correct volume – especially important for the standards!

qPCR is performed in triplicate. For ease, create a template, similar to Table 3 , that can be followed along with the qPCR plate. Table 3. 96-well plate layout template for 5-gene qPCR. (*16S standards will go from 10 3 – 10 8 .)
Carefully pipette 5 µL of PCR Water to the 3 non-template control (NTC) wells.
Add 15 µL of Master Mix to each utilized well on the 96-well plate.

Next, gently vortex to mix then pipette 5 µL of sample ( 5 µL of working stock; or 1 µL straight DNA + 4 µL PCR water) into wells, adding all replicates of one sample before starting the next. Use a new pipette tip for each replicate. To make a working stock dilution, see SOP: IDT Primer and gBlock Hydration and Aliquots .

Add 5 µL of gBlock standard (mix first by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip) starting with the lowest concentration of standard 10 1 /5µL, adding all triplicates of one dilution before starting the next dilution, to the final standard 10 8 /5µL. Use a new pipette tip for each replicate.
Seal the 96-well plate with optical tape, making sure all wells are completely covered.

Centrifuge the plate so that the entire volume is at the bottom of the well and no bubbles remain ( 1200 x g, °C, 1 minute ). Make sure a ‘Blank’ plate is in the other side of the centrifuge for balance.
Load the 96-well plate into the Real-Time Thermocycler.
Find the programmed gene conditions and double check that they match the conditions listed in Table 2 . %s %s
Run using a ‘FAM’ channel.
Run qPCR.
When cycle is complete: Export run onto a flash drive Discard the plate into the Biohazard bin. Turn off the Thermocycler.
Analyze the qPCR results ( Appendix B) go to step #18 .
Using the Plate Template created in Step 6 , label the wells in the ‘Plate Editor’ window ( Figure 1 ): Figure 1 . Plate Editor Window

On a computer with the CFX Maestro software, open the flash drive folder and find the file from the qPCR just run. This will open-up the CFX Maestro software. When prompted save the file within the Maestro program. Otherwise, find the icon and double click.
If not using the BioRad CFX, follow the guidelines of your machine’s system. Using a flash drive, export the data from the Thermocycler.
When the file opens, go to ‘Plate Setup’ dropdown in the top right corner and select ‘ View/Edit Plate .’
Highlight all wells and under Target Name ( 1 ) only select FAM.

Following the Plate Setup form, highlight the cells that contain sample, and under ‘ Technical Replicates ’ ( 3 ) select 3 for the ‘ Replicate Size ’ and make sure ‘ Horizontal ’ or ‘ Vertical ’ is selected based on how the template was created.

Highlight all wells in labeled ‘Unknown 1’, in the ‘ Sample Names ’ ( 5 ) dropdown select ‘Type Own Name’. Type the sample name/number and hit the ‘Enter’ key. Continue for the remaining number of samples.

Highlight the wells that contain Standards. Under ‘ Sample Type ’ ( 2 ) select ‘ Standards ’. These should appear as green banner on the well. Then go down to the ‘ Technical Replicates ’ ( 3 ) tab and put in the number 3 in the ‘ Replicate Size ’ box. Make sure ‘ Horizontal ’ or ‘ Vertical ’ is selected. Click Apply. A number 1-8 should appear next to the label on the banner.

Next, go to the ‘ Dilution Series ’ button underneath the ‘Technical Replicates’. Starting Concentration should be 1.00E+08 , and select ‘ Decreasing ’ or ‘ Increasing ’ based on the template created. Click apply. The dilution will appear in the white box underneath the label banner.
Highlight the Non-template Control wells, and label ‘ NTC ’ under Sample Type ( 2 ).
If any wells were unused, highlight and check the box ‘ Exclude Wells from Analysis ’ ( 4 ).
When all wells are labeled, click ‘ Trace Styles ’ ( 6 ) on the ribbon at the top of the window.

In the ‘ Trace Styles ’ window choose ‘ Random by Replicate ’. Then highlight the standards and select a color not used in the replicates, for example black color. Continue with the NTC (i.e. blue). This will make the standards stand out. Click ‘ Ok ’.
Back on the ‘Plate Editor’ page, click ‘ OK ’ ( 7 ) at the bottom of the page.
Then you will be taken back to the Main Data Analysis page ( Figure 2 ). Figure 2 . Data Analysis Page

Back on the main page, you will be in the ‘ Quantification tab ’ ( 1 ). Make sure the Standard Curve results (2) are within range ( Efficiency = 90-110%; R2≥ 0.98; Slope = -3.32 to -3.50 ) Check the box ‘Log Scale’ ( 3 )
In the ‘ Quantification Data ’ tab ( 4 ), you can see the Cq (quantitation cycle), Cq Mean, Standard Deviation and the SQ (starting quantity).

The Quantification Data can be exported using the ‘ Export ’ tab ( 5 ) on the top ribbon. There is an option for ‘ Custom Export ’ if needed. When ready, click ‘ Export’ , then save the file (this saved file will be a ‘comma file’. After it is saved, it can be re-saved as an excel file .
Save the whole Data Analysis file to a specified project folder.
creator: Justine C. Condon
